title: Dog Double Canine Teeth: Understanding Retained Baby Teeth
meta_desc: Does your dog have double canine teeth? Learn why retained baby teeth occur, why it is a low-urgency issue, and how a vet can help.
content_html:
What are Double Canine Teeth?

Double canine teeth occur when a puppy's deciduous (baby) tooth fails to fall out as the permanent adult tooth begins to erupt. Normally, the root of the baby tooth should be absorbed by the body, allowing it to loosen and fall out. When this doesn't happen, both teeth occupy the same socket, a condition technically known as retained deciduous teeth. This is particularly common in small and toy breed dogs.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for double canine teeth is Low. While this is not a medical emergency that requires an immediate trip to the ER, it should not be ignored. If the baby tooth is not removed, it can cause the adult tooth to grow in at an incorrect angle, leading to bite issues (malocclusion), pain, or early-onset periodontal disease because food and bacteria become trapped between the two teeth.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of your dog’s mouth can be very helpful for your veterinarian. A photo allows the vet to see the exact positioning of the teeth and check for any immediate signs of gum inflammation or trauma to the roof of the mouth. Providing a visual helps the clinical team determine if the extraction can wait until a scheduled spay/neuter procedure or if a separate dental appointment should be prioritized.

faqs.question: Will the baby tooth eventually fall out on its own?
faqs.answer: Once the adult tooth has fully emerged alongside the baby tooth, it is very unlikely the baby tooth will fall out naturally. The permanent tooth has already missed its chance to push the baby tooth out.

faqs.question: How are retained canine teeth treated?
faqs.answer: The standard treatment is surgical extraction of the retained baby tooth, usually performed under general anesthesia to ensure the entire root is removed without damaging the adult tooth.

faqs.question: At what age should I worry about double teeth?
faqs.answer: Most puppies should have all their adult teeth by 6 to 7 months of age. If you still see 'double' teeth at this age, you should schedule a veterinary consultation.
